items.original_candidate: Jane Billings of Brown University / Spelman College
items.new_candidate: Alma Jean Billingslea-Brown (Spelman College) [unverified for this specific ACCORD planning meeting]
items.evidence: Duncan's narrative places this person at a South Seas restaurant meeting circa 2002-2003 with David Nolan, JT Johnson, Cora Tyson Johnson, Dwight Hines, and Sandra Parks to plan the 40th anniversary of the St. Augustine civil rights movement, describing her as 'a professor at one of the schools. I think it was Spellman in Atlanta' — Duncan herself signals uncertainty. The original Pass 1 candidate parsed Whisper's 'Billings of Brown' as the institutional phrase 'Billings of Brown University' but no Spelman professor named 'Jane Billings' is documented at Brown or Spelman. Alma Jean Billingslea-Brown is a far better phonetic match: she is professor emerita and co-founder of African Diaspora Studies at Spelman, served as SCLC field staff 1967-1971 under Dr. King, and uses the hyphenated 'Billingslea-Brown' married surname — Whisper plausibly compressed 'Jean Billingslea-Brown' to 'Jane Billings of Brown.' However, no external source I located (Wikipedia, Spelman faculty bio, her academic CV, ACCORD's website, the ACCORD Freedom Trail timeline, or the Duncan-authored ACCORD PDF) directly places Billingslea-Brown at the South Seas meeting or in ACCORD's founding circle. Marked 'alternate' rather than 'confirmed/narrowed' because external corroboration of her specific St. Augustine involvement is missing; the Smithsonian-grade bar requires retaining the literal Whisper rendering as a fallback and flagging Billingslea-Brown as the leading hypothesis pending direct ACCORD-source confirmation (e.g., ACCORD's internal records, a Hayling-era oral history, or Duncan's own subsequent writings).

items.new_candidate: Fairchild (Fairchild Engine and Airplane Corporation / later Fairchild Industries)
items.evidence: The St. Augustine Historical Society and Northeast Florida Regional Airport (Wikipedia) both document that the aircraft modification plant at the St. Augustine airport site — the facility Northrop Grumman now operates — was originally announced and opened by Fairchild Engine and Airplane Corporation in 1954-1955, operated under the Fairchild name through the 1960s, and was referenced as Fairchild Industries by the time of its 1976-1977 closure. Grumman Corporation acquired the site in 1980; Northrop merged with Grumman in 1994 to form Northrop Grumman. Duncan's reference to 'the company that was previously there' aligns precisely with this single corporate lineage (Fairchild -> Grumman -> Northrop Grumman). The original Pass 1 candidate 'Fairchild Hiller' is a separate corporate entity — Fairchild Hiller Corporation was the helicopter-focused subsidiary formed by Fairchild's acquisition of Hiller Aircraft in 1964 — and is NOT the corporate name used at the St. Augustine site. The correct rendering is the generic 'Fairchild,' which is how the St. Augustine Historical Society and local sources refer to the predecessor operation. Whisper's 'Fertile Hill' is a phonetic mis-hearing of 'Fairchild.'
